page: Google Cloud Platform c4n-standard-2 is an x86_64 architecture server instance belonging to the c4n server family. It features 2 dedicated virtual CPUs paired with 7.0 GB of system memory, yielding a resource density of 3.5 GB of RAM per core. This instance does not include any integrated GPU hardware or local storage, meaning all storage requirements must be met via network-attached block storage. Additionally, it does not include any complimentary public IPv4 addresses.

Operating with dedicated CPU allocation, the instance provides predictable compute resources without the performance variability associated with shared-core or burstable configurations. However, the lack of local NVMe storage and integrated GPUs limits its utility for data-intensive storage operations or hardware-accelerated workloads. The 3.5 GB per vCPU ratio represents a balanced compute-to-memory allocation, positioning it between traditional compute-optimized and general-purpose profiles.

From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, the c4n-standard-2 offers an entry-point into the c4n family, allowing users to deploy dedicated x86_64 compute resources without paying for excess memory or unneeded local storage. This resource profile is appropriate for lightweight web serving, microservices, small-scale application hosting, and basic CI/CD pipeline tasks that require dedicated CPU execution but do not demand high memory capacity or local disk I/O.
description: Google Cloud Platform c4n-standard-2 is an x86_64 architecture server instance within the c4n family, configured with 2 dedicated vCPUs and 7.0 GB of system memory. This configuration provides a resource density of 3.5 GB of RAM per core. The instance does not feature local storage or integrated GPUs, requiring network-attached storage for data persistence. It also excludes complimentary public IPv4 addresses. With its dedicated CPU allocation, the c4n-standard-2 offers predictable compute performance, avoiding the resource contention typical of burstable instances. Qualitatively, it represents a cost-efficient option for workloads requiring dedicated processing power without high memory overhead. It is suitable for lightweight web serving, microservices, and basic development tasks.
